mappings.rationale: Directly covers Christianity including its history, beliefs, practices, and major forms — the primary subject of section 827.
Covers the life of Jesus Christ, directly relevant to 827.A.1.a on the origins of the primitive church and 827.F.1.c on Christology.
Covers the apostle Paul whose writings shaped early Christian theology, directly relevant to 827.A.1.a on the origins of the primitive church.
Augustine is central to the development of Christian theology, directly relevant to 827.A.2.b on the role of St. Augustine in doctrinal controversies.
Aquinas is a foundational Christian theologian, relevant to 827.C.1.b on religious and cultural characteristics of Latin Christianity and 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Anselm is a major medieval Christian theologian and philosopher, relevant to 827.C.1 on the medieval era and 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Covers the Bible as the foundational scripture of Christianity, directly relevant to 827.E.1 on biblical literature.
Covers the Old Testament, directly relevant to 827.E.1 on biblical literature including the Old Testament.
Directly covers the New Testament, the specific subject of 827.E.1 on biblical literature.
Examines the New Testament as literature, relevant to 827.E.1 on biblical literature.
Covers non-canonical early Christian texts, relevant to 827.E.1 on the Apocrypha.
Covers a foundational Anglican liturgical text, relevant to 827.G.3.d.iii on the Anglican Communion and 827.F.6.b on liturgy.
Covers Christian theology broadly, relevant to 827.E on intellectual expressions of Christianity and 827.F on beliefs and doctrines.
Directly covers Christian art and iconography, the subject of 827.F.7 on art and iconography.
Covers Christian ethical teaching and practice, relevant to 827.F.4 on doctrines concerning mankind and 827.F.6.c on forms of Christian life.
Directly covers Roman Catholicism, the subject of 827.G.2 on Roman Catholicism.
Directly covers Eastern Orthodoxy, the subject of 827.B on history of Eastern Orthodoxy and 827.G.1.
Directly covers Protestantism, the subject of 827.D on history of Protestantism and 827.G.3.
Directly covers the Protestant Reformation, the subject of 827.C.2 and 827.D.1.
Directly covers the Catholic Reformation and Counter-Reformation, the subject of 827.C.2.c and 827.D.1.g.
Covers Martin Luther's life and theology, directly relevant to 827.D.1.b on Luther and the German Reformation.
Covers Calvinism, directly relevant to 827.G.3.d.ii on Reformed and Presbyterian churches.
Directly covers Anglicanism, the subject of 827.G.3.d.iii on the Anglican Communion.
Covers Puritanism as a form of Protestant Christianity, relevant to 827.D.2.d-e on developments in English and American Protestantism.
Directly covers Pentecostalism, the subject of 827.G.3.e.ii on Pentecostal churches.
Directly covers Methodism, one of the Free churches listed in 827.G.3.d.iv.
Covers evangelicalism as a form of Protestantism, relevant to 827.G.3.e.vii on fundamentalist, evangelical, and other sectarian groups.
Directly covers the Society of Friends (Quakers), the subject of 827.G.3.e.iv.
Directly covers Mormonism, the subject of 827.H.3.
Covers the Book of Mormon as the foundational scripture of Mormonism, relevant to 827.H.3.
Covers Christian fundamentalism, relevant to 827.G.3.e.vii on fundamentalist groups.
Covers the Second Vatican Council, a landmark event in 20th-century Catholicism, relevant to 827.C.5 on developments in the 20th century.
The Crusades were a major expression of medieval Christianity, relevant to 827.C.1 on the medieval era of the Roman Catholic Church.
Covers devotion to the Virgin Mary across Christian traditions, relevant to 827.F on beliefs, practices, and institutions of Christianity.
Covers the relationship between Christianity and science, relevant to 827.A.2.c on the relation of Christian religion to culture.
Covers medieval Christian philosophy including scholasticism, relevant to 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy and 827.C.1.b on religious characteristics of Latin Christianity.
Covers the medieval period when Christianity dominated European culture, relevant to 827.C.1 on the medieval and Renaissance eras.
Covers the period of Christianity's establishment as the state religion, directly relevant to 827.A.2 on the early Christian Church from Constantine.
Covers Byzantine civilization and its relationship with Eastern Christianity, relevant to 827.B.1 on the church of imperial Byzantium.
Covers Christian monasticism, relevant to 827.F.6.c on monasticism as a form of Christian life.
Covers the Christian tradition of sainthood, relevant to 827.F.6.c on the saintly life.
Covers Christian martyrdom from the early church through modern times, relevant to 827.A.1.b on post-apostolic developments.
Covers the concept of miracles which is central to Christian belief, relevant to 827.F.1.c on Christology and Christian claims about Jesus.
Covers angelology in Christian tradition, relevant to 827.F.2 on doctrines concerning intermediary beings.
Covers the Christian concept of Satan, directly relevant to 827.F.2 on doctrines concerning Satan.
Covers Christian pilgrimage as a religious practice, relevant to 827.F.6 on practices common among Christians.
Covers biblical hermeneutics and interpretation theory, directly relevant to 827.E.2 on biblical exegesis and hermeneutics.
Kierkegaard is a major Christian philosopher and theologian, relevant to 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
C. S. Lewis was a major Christian apologist and writer, relevant to 827.E.5 on writings of theologians and church leaders.
Simone Weil was a significant Christian mystic and philosopher, relevant to 827.E.6 on Christian mysticism.
Covers Christian doctrines concerning the nature of God, relevant to 827.F.1 on doctrines concerning the nature and activity of God.
Covers Christianity in comparative perspective with Judaism and Islam, relevant to understanding Christianity's place among the Abrahamic traditions.
Covers Christianity in the American context, relevant to 827.D.2.e on American Protestantism and the various American denominational forms in 827.G.3.
Covers African American Christianity, relevant to 827.G.3.e.viii on variations of traditional Protestant faiths in black American society.
The Enlightenment challenged Christianity with rationalism and Deism, directly relevant to 827.D.2.c on the challenge of rationalism, Deism, and the Enlightenment.
The Renaissance shaped Christian art, culture, and the pre-Reformation church, relevant to 827.C.1 on the medieval and Renaissance eras.
Renaissance art is largely Christian in subject matter, relevant to 827.F.7 on major eras of Christian art.
Covers religious wars including the wars of religion of the Reformation era, relevant to 827.D.1.h on the wars of religion.
Dante's Divine Comedy is a supreme expression of medieval Christian theology and eschatology, relevant to 827.E and 827.F.1.d on eschatology.
Examines concepts of blasphemy in Christianity, relevant to 827.F on beliefs, practices, and institutions.
Covers the secularization of historically Christian societies, relevant to 827.D.4 on developments in Protestantism after WWI and the modern context.
The Holy Roman Empire's history is intertwined with Christianity's political role, relevant to 827.C.1.a on the papacy's temporal power and 827.D.1 on the Reformation.
Medieval literature is deeply shaped by Christian themes and theology, relevant to 827.E on intellectual expressions of Christianity.
The Norman Conquest reshaped the English church, relevant to 827.D.1.d on the English reformation's historical background.
Stuart Britain was shaped by religious conflicts between Puritans, Anglicans, and Catholics, relevant to 827.D.2.d on developments in English Protestantism.
Covers Christian spiritual traditions and mysticism, relevant to 827.E.6 on Christian mysticism.
Explores the nature of Christian faith and belief, relevant to 827.E.4 on creeds, dogmas, and confessions of faith.
Covers philosophical questions central to Christian theology including the existence and nature of God, relevant to 827.F.1.
Covers ritual practices including Christian liturgy and sacraments, relevant to 827.F.6.b on the role of liturgy.
Chaucer's Canterbury Tales engages with medieval Christian pilgrimage and church life, relevant to 827.C.1.b on religious and cultural characteristics of Latin Christianity.
Covers religious toleration in the context of Christian schisms and the wars of religion, relevant to 827.D.1.h on the development of religious liberty.
Covers Islamic civilization's interactions with Christianity including the Crusades, relevant to 827.C.1 on medieval Christianity.
Avicenna's philosophy significantly influenced medieval Christian theology and scholasticism, relevant to 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Covers decolonization which affected Christian missionary churches worldwide, relevant to 827.C.2.e on missionary endeavours.
Covers colonial American religion including Protestantism's development, relevant to 827.D.2.e on American Protestantism during the Colonial period.
